title: The Top 10 Herbal Teas for Reducing Premenstrual Syndrome Stress
intro_desc: Herbal teas for premenstrual syndrome are natural remedies that help alleviate symptoms associated with this condition, which can include mood swings, bloating, and fatigue. These teas, specifically Vitex agnus-castus, Passiflora incarnata, Zingiber officinale, Hypericum perforatum, Ginkgo biloba, Paeonia lactiflora, Cimicifuga racemosa, Lavandula angustifolia, Valeriana officinalis, and Angelica archangelica, work to regulate hormonal imbalances and reduce stress. Without proper treatment, premenstrual syndrome can severely impact daily life, causing emotional distress, relationship strain, and reduced productivity.

remedies.remedy_desc: Valeriana officinalis tea, also known as valerian root tea, is a natural remedy that can help with premenstrual syndrome (PMS). PMS is a common issue for many women, where they experience physical and emotional symptoms before their period. Valerian root tea can help with this because it contains a compound called valerenic acid, which has a calming effect on the body. This can help reduce symptoms such as anxiety, mood swings, and irritability that many women experience before their period. Additionally, valerian root tea can help with sleep, which is often disrupted in women with PMS. Drinking valerian root tea before bed can promote relaxation and improve sleep quality, leading to fewer symptoms of PMS. By reducing anxiety and promoting better sleep, valerian root tea can provide relief from the uncomfortable symptoms of PMS. It is also a natural and gentle remedy that can be used alongside other treatments to help manage PMS symptoms.

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Valeranone is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce pain and alleviate symptoms such as bloating and mood swings associated with PMS.

Valerenic acid is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has a calming effect on the nervous system, which helps to reduce symptoms of anxiety, depression, and mood swings often experienced during the menstrual cycle.

Valerenal is good for premenstrual syndrome because it helps to reduce anxiety and stress by acting on the brain's GABA system, resulting in a calming effect that can alleviate symptoms such as mood swings, irritability, and depression.
Isovaleric acid is good for premenstrual syndrome because it helps to reduce anxiety and mood swings by acting as a natural relaxant and balancing hormonal fluctuations.

remedies.image_alt: Valeriana officinalis herbal teas for premenstrual syndrome
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Angelica archangelica herbal tea for premenstrual syndrome (PMS), start by gathering the necessary ingredients: dried Angelica root, a tea infuser or a piece of cheesecloth, and a pot of boiling water. Begin by adding one teaspoon of dried Angelica root to the tea infuser or cheesecloth, then pour boiling water over it. Allow the mixture to steep for 5-7 minutes, or until the liquid has reduced slightly and the flavors have been released. Strain the tea into a cup, discarding the solids, and add honey or lemon to taste if desired. For PMS relief, it's recommended to drink 1-2 cups of Angelica tea per day, ideally starting a few days before your period is expected to begin and continuing throughout your menstrual cycle. This herbal tea is said to help alleviate symptoms such as bloating, cramps, and mood swings, making it a natural and soothing addition to your self-care routine.

remedies.remedy_desc: Zingiber officinale, also known as ginger, is a popular spice used in cooking and herbal teas. It has been used for centuries to help with various health issues. For women who experience premenstrual syndrome (PMS), ginger tea can be a helpful remedy. PMS is a set of physical and emotional symptoms that occur before a woman's period, including mood swings, bloating, and cramps. Ginger tea has anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce cramps and ease bloating. It also has a compound called gingerol, which has been shown to have a positive effect on serotonin levels, helping to improve mood and reduce anxiety. Additionally, ginger tea has been known to help with digestion and nausea, which are common symptoms of PMS. Drinking ginger tea regularly may also help to reduce the severity of PMS symptoms.

remedy_constituents.constituent_desc: Gingerol is good for premenstrual syndrome because it helps to reduce inflammation and ease symptoms such as cramping, bloating, and mood swings, by acting as a natural pain reliever and anti-inflammatory agent.

Shogaol is good for premenstrual syndrome because it helps to reduce anxiety and inflammation in the body, which can ease symptoms like mood swings and breast tenderness often experienced during this time.
Zingiberene is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has anti-inflammatory properties that help reduce bloating and cramps associated with PMS, promoting a sense of relaxation and comfort.

remedies.image_alt: Zingiber officinale herbal teas for premenstrual syndrome
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make an herbal tea from Angelica archangelica that can help with premenstrual syndrome (PMS), start by gathering a handful of dried or fresh Angelica roots. If you're using fresh roots, dry them in a low-temperature oven (150-200°F) for 1-2 hours to preserve their properties. Once your Angelica is ready, place 1-2 teaspoons of the dried roots in a teapot or infuser. Pour 1 cup of boiling water over the Angelica, then let it steep for 5-7 minutes. As the tea steeps, the Angelica's active compounds, such as angelic acid and ligustilide, will release into the water, creating a soothing and calming brew. After the steeping time is up, strain the tea into a cup and enjoy it hot or cold, sweetened with honey if desired. Drink 1-2 cups of the Angelica tea a day to help alleviate PMS symptoms like bloating, cramps, and mood swings. Note that while Angelica tea can be beneficial, it's essential to consult with a healthcare professional before using it as a treatment for PMS, especially if you're pregnant or breastfeeding.

remedies.remedy_desc: Vitex agnus-castus tea has been used for centuries to help with premenstrual syndrome (PMS). PMS is a common condition that affects many women, causing symptoms like mood swings, breast tenderness, and bloating. Vitex agnus-castus tea is thought to work by regulating hormones in the body, specifically estrogen and progesterone. When these hormones are out of balance, it can lead to PMS symptoms. The tea is believed to help the body produce more progesterone, which can help to reduce symptoms like mood swings and breast tenderness. Some women also find that Vitex agnus-castus tea helps to reduce symptoms like anxiety and depression, which are common during PMS. Additionally, the tea may help to regulate menstrual cycles, making it easier to predict when periods will start. Many women have reported relief from PMS symptoms after drinking Vitex agnus-castus tea regularly. The tea is often consumed as a supplement to other treatments, and some women choose to drink it on its own.

Iridoids are good for premenstrual syndrome because they have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that help to reduce the symptoms of PMS, such as bloating, cramps, and mood swings.

Phenolic acids are good for premenstrual syndrome because they have anti-inflammatory properties that help to reduce swelling and pain in the body, which can help to alleviate symptoms of PMS such as bloating, cramps, and mood swings.

Triterpenoids are good for premenstrual syndrome because they have anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce anxiety and alleviate symptoms such as bloating and mood swings associated with PMS.
Roscmarinic acid is good for premenstrual syndrome because it has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, which help to reduce stress, anxiety, and other symptoms associated with PMS.

remedies.image_alt: Vitex agnus-castus herbal teas for premenstrual syndrome
remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Angelica archangelica herbal tea that helps with premenstrual syndrome, start by gathering dried Angelica roots. You can purchase these roots from a health food store or online. Next, take one teaspoon of the dried roots and place it in a tea infuser or a heat-resistant cup. Pour boiling water over the roots, making sure to cover them completely. Let the mixture steep for 5-7 minutes, allowing the roots to release their medicinal properties. After the steeping time has passed, carefully remove the tea infuser or cup from the heat. Strain the tea into a separate container to remove the roots, if needed. Your Angelica archangelica tea is now ready to drink. It is recommended to drink 1-2 cups of the tea per day, 2-3 days before your period, to help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome such as bloating, cramps, and mood swings. You can also add honey or lemon to the tea if desired for taste.

remedies.remedy_desc: Cimicifuga racemosa, also known as black cohosh, is a plant used to make tea that can help with premenstrual syndrome (PMS). Many women find relief from symptoms like mood swings, bloating, and cramps when they drink Cimicifuga racemosa tea. This is because the tea has natural chemicals that can help calm the body and mind. It can reduce inflammation, which may be causing the cramps and other symptoms. The tea can also help regulate hormones, which may be contributing to the PMS symptoms. Additionally, Cimicifuga racemosa tea has been shown to have a positive effect on the body's production of serotonin, a chemical that helps with mood and sleep. Some studies suggest that drinking Cimicifuga racemosa tea may reduce the severity of PMS symptoms and improve the quality of life for women who experience them. The tea may also be helpful for women who are experiencing menopausal symptoms, as it can help alleviate hot flashes and night sweats.

remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Angelica archangelica herbal tea that helps with premenstrual syndrome, start by gathering the necessary ingredients. You will need dried Angelica root or fresh Angelica roots, which can be obtained from a health food store or grown in your garden. If using fresh roots, clean and chop them into small pieces. Next, boil a cup of water and add one teaspoon of dried Angelica root or one tablespoon of chopped fresh Angelica roots to the boiling water. Reduce the heat to a simmer and let it steep for 5-7 minutes. You can also add other herbs like peppermint or chamomile to enhance the flavor and benefits of the tea. Once the tea has cooled down, strain it into a cup and enjoy it. It's recommended to drink one to two cups of Angelica tea a day, ideally one week before the onset of menstruation. This herbal tea is known to help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome, such as bloating, mood swings, and cramps, due to its anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic properties.

remedies.remedy_desc: Lavandula angustifolia tea, also known as English lavender, is a herbal remedy that can help with premenstrual syndrome (PMS). This tea contains a chemical called linalool, which is known to reduce stress and anxiety. Many women who experience PMS often feel anxious and irritable before their period, and linalool can help calm their nerves. Additionally, Lavandula angustifolia tea has a soothing effect on the body, which can help to ease cramps and bloating that often come with PMS. The tea also contains antispasmodic properties, which can help to relax the muscles and reduce cramp pain. Some women also report that Lavandula angustifolia tea helps to improve their sleep quality, which is often disrupted during the premenstrual phase. Drinking this tea may also help to regulate hormone levels, which can contribute to PMS symptoms.

remedies.remedy_desc: Paeonia lactiflora tea is a natural remedy that may help with premenstrual syndrome (PMS) symptoms. This tea is made from the root of the peony flower, which has been used for centuries in traditional Chinese medicine. The root contains a compound called paeoniflorin, which has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. These properties may help to reduce anxiety and depression, two common symptoms of PMS. Paeonia lactiflora tea may also help to regulate hormones and reduce bloating and cramps. Additionally, the tea has been shown to have a calming effect on the nervous system, which may help to reduce irritability and mood swings. Drinking Paeonia lactiflora tea may also help to improve sleep quality, which is often disrupted during PMS. As a result, women who drink this tea may find that their PMS symptoms are less severe, and they are able to manage them more effectively. The tea is also thought to have a balancing effect on the body's energy, which may help to reduce fatigue and other symptoms of PMS.

remedies.remedy_preparation: To make Angelica archangelica herbal tea that helps with premenstrual syndrome, start by gathering fresh or dried Angelica roots. Choose roots with a thick, white or pale yellow skin and a sturdy stem. Clean and dry the roots thoroughly to prevent any dirt or moisture from affecting the tea's quality. For a single serving, combine one teaspoon of Angelica roots with one cup of boiling water in a tea infuser or a heat-resistant cup. Let the roots steep for 5-7 minutes to allow the herbal properties to release into the water. Strain the tea and discard the roots. You can add honey or lemon to taste, but it's essential to consume the tea without any added sugars or spices to experience its full benefits. Drinking Angelica archangelica tea regularly, ideally 1-2 cups per day, may help alleviate PMS symptoms such as bloating, mood swings, and cramps. However, consult with a healthcare professional before using it as a replacement for medical treatment.

remedies.remedy_desc: Melissa officinalis tea, also known as lemon balm tea, is a popular herbal remedy for premenstrual syndrome (PMS). This tea is rich in antioxidants and has anti-inflammatory properties, which can help to soothe and calm the body. The active compounds in lemon balm, such as rosmarinic acid and citral, have been shown to reduce anxiety and stress levels, which are common symptoms of PMS. By promoting relaxation and reducing tension, Melissa officinalis tea can help to alleviate symptoms like mood swings, irritability, and restlessness. Additionally, the tea's calming effect can also help to improve sleep quality, which is often disrupted during the premenstrual phase. Furthermore, lemon balm tea has been traditionally used to ease menstrual cramps and other physical symptoms associated with PMS, making it a natural and effective remedy for women seeking relief from these symptoms.

You can find them in health stores or online. Rinse the roots under cold running water, then chop them into small pieces. Next, add one teaspoon of the chopped roots to a cup of boiling water. remedies.remedy_desc: Hypericum perforatum tea, also known as St. John's Wort, is a natural remedy that can help ease symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS). Many women find that it reduces breast tenderness and swelling, which are common complaints during PMS. The tea's anti-inflammatory properties also help to soothe mood swings and irritability, making it easier to manage stress and anxiety. Additionally, St. John's Wort has been shown to have a balancing effect on hormones, which can help regulate menstrual cycles and reduce symptoms of PMS. Some women also use the tea to alleviate symptoms of depression and anxiety, which are common co-occurring conditions with PMS. The tea's active compounds, such as hyperforin and hypericin, have been found to have a positive impact on neurotransmitters, which play a crucial role in mood regulation. Drinking Hypericum perforatum tea may also help to improve sleep quality, which is often disrupted during PMS.

Begin by boiling one cup of water in a pot. Next, add a teaspoon of dried Angelica root and a teaspoon of dried peppermint leaves to the boiling water. supplementary_best: To help alleviate symptoms of premenstrual syndrome (PMS), a combination of herbal teas can be effective. A blend of 'Angelica archangelica', 'Melissa officinalis', 'Vitex agnus-castus', and 'Paeonia lactiflora' is a good option.  'Angelica archangelica' helps to reduce bloating and water retention, which are common PMS symptoms. 'Melissa officinalis', also known as lemon balm, has a calming effect, which can help with anxiety and mood swings.  'Vitex agnus-castus' or chasteberry is known to regulate menstrual cycles and reduce symptoms of PMS. It helps to balance estrogen levels, which can become imbalanced before a period, leading to mood swings, breast tenderness, and other symptoms.  'Paeonia lactiflora', also known as peony root, is a natural sedative and anti-inflammatory. It helps to reduce menstrual cramps and promote relaxation.  This combination is effective because it addresses multiple symptoms of PMS, including anxiety, bloating, menstrual cramps, and mood swings. It also helps to regulate menstrual cycles and balance estrogen levels.
supplementary_avoid: If you have premenstrual syndrome (PMS), there are some herbal teas you should avoid. One such tea is 'Ephedra sinica', also known as ma huang. This tea can increase blood pressure and heart rate, which may not be suitable for people with PMS who may already experience mood swings and other symptoms.  Another tea to avoid is 'Ginkgo biloba'. While it is often used to improve memory and concentration, it can also increase the risk of bleeding, which may be a concern for people with PMS who may be prone to heavy bleeding during their periods.  Also, 'Glycyrrhiza glabra', or licorice root tea, is not recommended for people with PMS. It can cause blood pressure to rise and may interact with medications, such as blood thinners.  Additionally, 'Silybum marianum', or milk thistle tea, should be avoided by people with PMS who are taking estrogen replacement therapy. This is because milk thistle may decrease the effectiveness of estrogen replacement therapy.  Finally, 'Taraxacum officinale', or dandelion tea, can cause stomach upset and interact with medications, such as blood thinners and diabetes medications, which may be a concern for people with PMS who may already experience gastrointestinal symptoms and other health issues.
supplementary_other_remedies: Magnesium and vitamin B6 supplements can help alleviate premenstrual syndrome (PMS) symptoms. Magnesium helps regulate the body's water balance and can reduce cramps and bloating. It also has a calming effect, which can help ease anxiety and irritability. Vitamin B6, on the other hand, plays a crucial role in hormone regulation and can help reduce symptoms like mood swings and breast tenderness. Some people also find relief in taking omega-3 fatty acid supplements, which have anti-inflammatory properties and can help reduce inflammation and pain associated with PMS. Additionally, applying warm compresses or taking a warm bath can help relax the muscles and improve blood flow, reducing cramps and discomfort. Aromatherapy with essential oils like lavender and chamomile can also promote relaxation and calmness, helping to reduce stress and anxiety associated with PMS.
supplementary_related_ailments: People with premenstrual syndrome (PMS) often experience a range of symptoms beyond just mood swings and cramps. These can include headaches due to hormonal changes and inflammation, which can be alleviated with feverfew, a herb known to reduce prostaglandins that cause pain. Dizziness and lightheadedness may be caused by estrogen fluctuations, and ginger, with its anti-inflammatory properties, can help stabilize blood flow and reduce dizziness. Many women with PMS also experience bloating and water retention, which can be treated with dandelion root, a natural diuretic that helps the body eliminate excess fluids. Some people may experience skin issues like acne and dark spots, which can be improved with the use of saw palmetto, a herb that helps regulate androgen hormones that contribute to skin problems. Additionally, PMS can lead to digestive issues like constipation or diarrhea, which can be soothed with peppermint, a herb that eases muscle spasms in the digestive tract and promotes relaxation. These medicinal herbs work by addressing the underlying hormonal and inflammatory imbalances that contribute to PMS symptoms, providing a natural and effective way to manage these issues.
